title: Classes & Objects
subtitle: Build your own blueprints — and stamp out objects from them like cookies!
goal: Understand what a class and an object are, write a class with __init__, give it attributes and methods, and create instances you can use.

blocks.heading: A class is a blueprint 🧱
blocks.html:
Today we learn Object-Oriented Programming (OOP for short). Don't let the big name scare you — the idea is simple and cozy.

Imagine a cookie cutter 🍪. The cutter itself is not a cookie. It's a shape — a plan for making cookies. In Python, that plan is called a class.

When you press the cutter into dough, you get a real cookie you can eat. That real cookie is called an object (or an instance). One cutter can make a hundred cookies, and each cookie is its own thing.

So remember: class = blueprint, object = the thing built from it.

blocks.heading: Writing your first class
blocks.html:
We make a class with the word class and a name that starts with a Capital letter. Inside it we put a special setup function called __init__ (that's two underscores on each side).

__init__ runs automatically every time we make a new object. It's where we say what each object should remember — those remembered values are called attributes.

blocks.caption: A Dog blueprint, and one real dog
blocks.explain: Dog is the blueprint. my_dog = Dog("Rex") stamps out one real dog. __init__ stored the name as an attribute (self.name). bark() is a method — a function that belongs to the dog — and we call it with my_dog.bark().

blocks.heading: What is this 'self' thing? 🤔
blocks.html:
You'll see self everywhere inside a class. Here's the gentle truth: self means "this particular object."

When you write my_dog.bark(), Python quietly passes my_dog in as self. So inside the method, self.name means "this dog's name." If you had a second dog, its self.name would be different. Each object keeps its own attributes.
Two dogs, two names. Make as many objects as you like from one class. a = Dog("Rex") and b = Dog("Bella") are totally separate — changing one never touches the other.

blocks.caption: Many objects from one blueprint
blocks.explain: One class, two objects. rex and bella each remember their own name, so they bark differently. That's the power of a blueprint.

blocks.question: In the cookie analogy, which one is the class?
blocks.explain: The class is the blueprint — the cookie cutter. The actual cookie you make from it is the object (instance).

blocks.heading: Methods that change attributes
blocks.html: Methods aren't just for printing. They can change an object's attributes too. Let's build a Player who starts with a score of 0 and can earn points.

blocks.caption: A Player who gains points
blocks.code:
class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.score = 0

    def add_points(self, points):
        self.score = self.score + points
        print(self.name + " now has " + str(self.score) + " points!")

hero = Player("Maya")
hero.add_points(10)
hero.add_points(5)
print("Final score:", hero.score)
blocks.explain: __init__ gave every new Player a name and a starting score of 0. The add_points method updates self.score, so the player remembers their points between calls.

blocks.html: Heads up: the first parameter of every method is almost always self. You write it in the definition, but you do NOT pass it yourself when calling. hero.add_points(10) sends only the 10 — Python adds self for you.

blocks.question: What does __init__ do?
options: It runs automatically when you create an object, to set up its attributes
blocks.explain: __init__ is the constructor. It runs the moment you make a new object and sets up its starting attributes like self.name.

blocks.question: Inside a method, what does self.score mean?
options: The score of every object at once
A score that all objects share
blocks.explain: self means 'this object', so self.score is the score belonging to the specific object the method was called on.

blocks.title: Make a Cat that meows
blocks.instructions: Fill in the blanks so the Cat class stores a name and has a meow() method that prints the cat's name followed by ' says Meow!'. Then make a cat and call meow().
blocks.failMsg: Set self.name = name inside __init__, then call kitty.meow() so it prints 'says Meow!'.

blocks.title: Give the Player a method to lose points
blocks.instructions: The Player class below can add points. Add a lose_points method that subtracts points from self.score, then call it. Make the final score print out.
blocks.starter:
class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.score = 20

    def add_points(self, points):
        self.score = self.score + points

    # write lose_points(self, points) below

p = Player("Sam")
p.add_points(10)
# call lose_points here
print("Score:", p.score)
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your method updates the object's own score. That's real OOP!
blocks.failMsg: Add a method lose_points(self, points) that does self.score = self.score - points, then call p.lose_points(5).

challenge.title: Build a BankAccount class
challenge.instructions: Make a class called BankAccount. In __init__, store an owner name and start the balance at 0. Add a deposit(amount) method that adds to the balance, and a withdraw(amount) method that subtracts from it. After each action, print the new balance. Then create an account, deposit some money, withdraw some, and show the final balance.
challenge.starter:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = 0

    def deposit(self, amount):
        # add amount to self.balance, then print the balance
        pass

    # add a withdraw(self, amount) method too

acc = BankAccount("Alex")
acc.deposit(100)
# withdraw some money below
print("Final balance:", acc.balance)
challenge.solution:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = 0

    def deposit(self, amount):
        self.balance = self.balance + amount
        print(self.owner + "'s balance: " + str(self.balance))

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        self.balance = self.balance - amount
        print(self.owner + "'s balance: " + str(self.balance))

acc = BankAccount("Alex")
acc.deposit(100)
acc.withdraw(30)
print("Final balance:", acc.balance)
challenge.hint: deposit should do self.balance = self.balance + amount. withdraw is the same but with a minus. Both should print the balance after changing it.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Incredible! You built a working bank with your own class. You now understand classes, objects, attributes, and methods — the heart of OOP. Day 18 complete!
